title: Why AlexNet Beat Older Vision Pipelines
concept: AlexNet beat older vision pipelines because it learned feature hierarchies directly from pixels at large scale, instead of depending on hand-crafted descriptors plus a separate classifier.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Which AlexNet choice helped it avoid memorizing the training set too narrowly?
exercises.explain: Right — those choices helped AlexNet generalize better.

cards.title: Stop hand-designing every clue
cards.body: Before deep CNNs took over, many vision systems followed a recipe like this: hand-craft visual descriptors first, then train a separate classifier on top. AlexNet showed that learning features end to end from pixels could work better at large scale.

cards.title: What older pipelines looked like
cards.body: An older vision pipeline often used hand-crafted features such as edges, corners, gradients, or texture descriptors designed by humans, then fed those fixed features into a classifier. AlexNet instead learned multiple layers of features from data as part of one CNN.

cards.title: Cookie cutters vs. learning new shapes
cards.body: Hand-crafted features are like bringing a fixed box of cookie cutters and hoping the right shapes are already inside. A learned CNN is more flexible: it can discover new useful shapes from the training examples instead of depending only on what humans guessed in advance.

cards.title: Why scale matters
cards.body: If you only need to separate a few easy classes, hand-crafted descriptors can work surprisingly well. But across ImageNet's 1000 categories, the useful clues become too varied. AlexNet could build a hierarchy from simple edges to object parts to whole-object patterns, which scales better than one fixed feature toolkit.

cards.title: Older pipelines were not silly
cards.body: It is tempting to mock pre-deep-learning vision, but those pipelines were strong for their time. The real change was that large datasets and GPU training finally made learned feature hierarchies more powerful than hand-designed ones on broad benchmarks like ImageNet.

cards.title: Two different ways to build vision systems
cards.body: The key contrast is where the features come from.

left.text:
Human-designed descriptors first
Separate classifier second
Harder to scale across many categories

right.text:
Learn features from pixels
Many layers build a hierarchy
End-to-end learning at scale

exercises.prompt: Which description matches an older hand-crafted vision pipeline?

options.text: Design descriptors first, then train a classifier on those fixed features
Learn all feature stages jointly from pixels inside one deep CNN
Use no features and no classifier at all
Only label images after the model predicts them

exercises.explain: Exactly — older systems often separated feature design from final classification.

exercises.prompt: True or false: AlexNet's advantage came partly from learning feature hierarchies directly from data instead of relying only on fixed hand-crafted descriptors.
exercises.explain: True — that is one of the core differences between AlexNet and older pipelines.

exercises.sentence: Instead of hand-crafting features first, AlexNet learned them directly from ___.
exercises.explain: Correct — AlexNet learned filters directly from image pixels and labels.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best explains why AlexNet scaled better to ImageNet's many categories?

options.text: Its deep CNN could learn many useful feature stages from data instead of depending on one fixed human-designed descriptor set
It solved scale by shrinking ImageNet to only a few classes
It ignored visual patterns and predicted labels alphabetically

exercises.explain: Right — large, varied category sets reward models that can discover richer features from data.

exercises.prompt: A learner says, 'Why would AlexNet beat a carefully engineered older vision pipeline on ImageNet?' Which answer is best?

options.text: Because AlexNet learned a deep hierarchy of features from pixels at large scale, while older pipelines depended on fixed hand-crafted descriptors that were harder to stretch across 1000 categories
Because AlexNet removed the need for training data and labels
Because older pipelines were illegal in competitions after 2012
Because hand-crafted pipelines always fail, even on small tasks

exercises.explain: Exactly. AlexNet's edge came from learned feature hierarchies at scale, not from magically making older methods useless everywhere. Next: what happened to the field after that win.
